analogy: Think of "Rights" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 5 Rights In everyday life we often talk of our rights. As members of a democratic country we may speak of such rights as the right to vote, the right to form political parties, the right to contest elections and so on. But apart... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Rights is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that chapter 5 Rights In everyday life we often talk of our rights.

The text explains that as members of a democratic country we may speak of such rights as the right to vote, the right to form political parties, the right to contest elections and so on.

The text explains that but apart from the generally accepted political and civil rights, people today are also making new demands for rights such as the right to information, right to clean air or the right to safe drinking water.

The text explains that rights are claimed not only in relation to our political and public lives but also in relation to our social and personal relationships.

The text explains that moreover, rights may be claimed not only for adult human beings but also for children, unborn foetuses, and even animals.

The text explains that the notion of rights is thus invoked in a variety of different ways by different people.

The text explains that in this chapter we will explore: o What do we mean when we speak of rights? o What is the basis on which rights are claimed? o What purpose do rights serve and, why are they so important?

The text explains that rights Rights 68 Political Theory 5.1 WHAT ARE RIGHTS?

The text explains that a right is essentially an entitlement or a justified claim.

The text explains that it denotes what we are entitled to as citizens, as individuals and as human beings.

The text explains that it is something that we consider to be due to us; something that the rest of society must recognise as being a legitimate claim that must be upheld.

The text explains that this does not mean that everything that I regard to be necessary and desirable is a right.

The text explains that i may want to wear the clothes of my choice to school rather than the prescribed uniform.

The text explains that i may want to stay out late at night but this does not mean that I have a right to dress in any way I like at school or to return home when I choose to do so.

The text explains that there is a distinction between what I want and think I am entitled to, and what can be designated as rights.

The text explains that rights are primarily those claims that I along with others regard to be necessary for leading a life of respect and dignity.

The text explains that in fact, one of the grounds on which rights have been claimed is that they represent conditions that we collectively see as a source of self- respect and dignity.

The text explains that however, in most cases the claimed rights are directed towards the state.

The text explains that rightsRightsPolitical Theory 77 On 10 December 1948, the General Assembly of the United Nations adopted and proclaimed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

The text explains that following this historic act the Assembly called upon all Member countries to publicise the text of the Declaration and “to cause it to be disseminated, displayed, read and expounded principally in schools and other educational institutions, without distinction based on the political status of countries or territories.” PREAMBLE Whereas recognition of the inherent dignity and of the equal and inalienable rights of all members of the human family is the foundation of freedom, justice and peace in the world, Whereas disregard and contempt for human rights have resulted in barbarous acts which have outraged the conscience of mankind, and the advent of a world in which human beings shall enjoy freedom of speech and belief and freedom from fear and want has been proclaimed as the highest aspiration of the common people, Whereas it is essential, if man is not to be compelled to have recourse, as a last resort, to rebellion against tyranny and oppression, that human rights should be protected by the rule of law, Whereas it is essential to promote the development of friendly relations between nations, Whereas the peoples of the United Nations have in the Charter reaffirmed their faith in fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person and in the equal rights of men and women and have determined to promote social progress and better standards of life in larger freedom, Whereas Member States have pledged themselves to achieve, in co-operation with the United Nations, the promotion of universal respect for and observance of human rights and fundamental freedoms, Whereas a common understanding of these rights and freedoms is of the greatest importance for the full realisation of this pledge, Now, therefore THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY proclaims THIS UNIVERSAL DECLARATION OF HUMAN RIGHTS as a common standard of achievement for all peoples and all nations, to the end that every individual and every organ of society, keeping this Declaration constantly in mind, shall strive by teaching and education to promote respect for these rights and freedoms and by progressive measures, national and international, to secure their universal and effective recognition and observance, both among the peoples of Member States themselves and among the peoples of territories under their jurisdiction.

The text explains that rights Rights 78 Political Theory Exercises 1.

The text explains that what are the bases on which claims to rights can be made?

The text explains that on what grounds are some rights considered to be universal in nature?

The text explains that identify three rights which you consider universal.

The text explains that discuss briefly some of the new rights claims which are being put forward in our country today — for example the rights of tribal peoples to protect their habitat and way of life, or the rights of children against bonded labour.

The text explains that differentiate between political, economic and cultural rights.

The text explains that rights place some limits on the authority of the state.

The text explains that credit: Image on opening page: The National Archives and Records Administration, United States of America

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 5: Rights

prelims_traps: Do not treat 5.1 WHAT ARE RIGHTS? and 5.2 WHERE DO RIGHTS COME FROM? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Rights; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Rights through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 5.1 WHAT ARE RIGHTS? and 5.2 WHERE DO RIGHTS COME FROM? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
